data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness filtering is fundamentally a variance-reduction strategy that strictly beats self-consistency only when the k-candidate sample exhibits a high rate of near-tie or non-unique plurality (ambiguity rate >= 25%), and the critic preferentially breaks ties toward the correct answer with probability > 0.65; in all other regimes it is neutral or harmful. The observed data (deltas of exactly 0.00) are explained by zero ambiguity rate: at B=0.01-0.033 and k=5, almost every question has a unique 4/5 or 5/0 plurality, leaving no ties for the critic to break.","mechanism": "Self-consistency resolves ties arbitrarily (random tie-break). When the modal answer is a 2-2-1 or 3-2 split, SC is at chance on the tie. Harness filtering, by removing one or more candidates, can break a 3-2 wrong-majority into a 2-2 tie (no worse than SC) or a 2-1 correct-majority (strictly better), and can break a 2-2 tie into a 2-1 (strictly better if the survivor is correct). The strict advantage comes exclusively from tie-breaking. When there are no ties (unique 4/5 or 5/0 plurality), filtering can only destroy a correct majority or leave a wrong majority unchanged (if it removes the wrong candidate) — never improve. The loss condition is when the critic breaks ties toward the wrong answer (probability < 0.5), which happens when critic error is anti-correlated with candidate error.","scope": {"covers": ["k=3 to k=10", "base error 0.10-0.50 (where ties are common under binomial sampling)", "ambiguity rate (non-unique plurality) >= 0.15", "random tie-breaking in SC baseline"], "excludes": ["k >= 20 (ties become vanishingly rare under low B; CLT dominates)", "base error < 0.05 (plurality is almost always unique and correct, no ties to break)", "deterministic tie-breaking in SC (changes baseline)", "base error > 0.5 with two-answer format (plurality always wrong, no beneficial tie to break)"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "At k=5, B=0.30, ambiguity rate >= 0.25, critic tie-break-correct probability >= 0.65, but harness does not strictly beat SC by >= 0.03 (would refute the tie-breaking sufficiency claim)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "At k=5, B=0.30, ambiguity rate = 0 (all unique pluralities), but harness strictly beats SC by > 0.02 (would refute: ties are necessary for improvement)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "At k=5, B=0.30, ambiguity rate >= 0.25, critic tie-break-correct probability <= 0.45, but harness does not show strict loss vs SC by >= 0.02 (would refute: anti-aligned tie-breaking is harmful)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Ambiguity rate alone (without critic tie-break accuracy) predicts harness-vs-SC delta with R-squared > 0.5 across all 12 cells (would refute: tie-break accuracy is also necessary)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}],"prose_notes": "This is an atypical but testable reframing: harness is not about filtering wrong answers but about breaking ties. It predicts that the marginal precision/recall regime is a red herring — what matters is the joint distribution of plurality ties and critic alignment. The experiment explicitly controls for ambiguity by selecting questions that produce ties at k=5. The key risk: if SC uses deterministic rather than random tie-breaking, the baseline changes and the tie-breaking advantage may shrink."}
